model_narrative: We ship a product from several canning plants to several markets. For each plant and market we decide how much to ship, and because the per-unit transport cost falls as the shipped quantity grows, we also decide how that quantity is placed along a piecewise-linear cost curve by choosing which segment of the curve is active and how far into it the shipment reaches. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost across all plant-market pairs.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the supply locations, each a canning plant that ships product out

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand locations, each a market that must receive product

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of the piecewise-linear cost curve, listed from the lowest to the highest shipment level

sets.name: g
sets.doc: the segments of the piecewise-linear cost curve, one per interval between consecutive breakpoints; a subset of the breakpoints that excludes the final one

params.name: a
params.doc: the shipping capacity of each plant, in cases; the most that plant can send out in total

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand at each market, in cases; the least that market must receive in total

params.name: c
params.doc: the transport cost coefficient for each plant-market pair, in thousands of dollars, applied per unit of the square-root shipment measure

params.name: p
params.doc: the shipment quantity at each breakpoint of the cost curve, in cases; the x-coordinate of that breakpoint

params.name: nseg
params.doc: the width of each segment along the shipment axis, in cases; the gap in shipment quantity between a segment's breakpoint and the next breakpoint

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the shipment quantity sent from a plant to a market, in cases

vars.name: sqrtx
vars.doc: the square-root measure of the shipment quantity for a plant-market pair, used to price the concave economy-of-scale cost

vars.name: seg
vars.doc: the amount of shipment placed within a given segment of the cost curve for a plant-market pair, as a fraction-like fill of that segment

vars.name: gs
vars.doc: the segment-selection indicator for a plant-market pair; equals 1 if that segment is the active one and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all plant-market pairs, in thousands of dollars

description: Each plant cannot ship out more than it is able to. For every plant, the total shipment sent from it across all markets must not exceed that plant's shipping capacity.

description: For each plant and market, the shipment quantity must equal the level reconstructed from the cost-curve segments. Add up, over all segments, the shipment level at each segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is the active one, plus the segment width scaled by how far the shipment reaches into that segment, and set the shipment quantity equal to that total.

model_narrative: We ship a product from several canning plants to several markets. For each plant and market we decide how much to ship, and because the per-unit transport cost falls as the shipped quantity grows, we also decide how that quantity is placed along a piecewise-linear cost curve by choosing which segment of the curve is active and how far into it the shipment reaches. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost across all plant-market pairs.

sets.name: s

sets.name: g
sets.doc: the segments of the piecewise-linear cost curve, one per interval between consecutive breakpoints; a subset of the breakpoints that excludes the final one

params.name: a

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the shipment quantity sent from a plant to a market, in cases

vars.name: sqrtx

vars.name: seg

vars.name: gs
vars.doc: the segment-selection indicator for a plant-market pair; equals 1 if that segment is the active one and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all plant-market pairs, in thousands of dollars

description: To build the complete model, enforce the following relationships in order. First, each plant cannot ship out more than it is able to. For every plant, the total shipment sent from it across all markets must not exceed that plant's shipping capacity. Second, every market must have its demand met. For each market, the total shipment arriving into it from all plants must be at least that market's required amount. Third, for each plant and market, the shipment quantity must equal the level reconstructed from the cost-curve segments. Add up, over all segments, the shipment level at each segment's starting breakpoint counted when that segment is the active one, plus the segment width scaled by how far the shipment reaches into that segment, and set the shipment quantity equal to that total. Fourth, a segment can only be filled if it is the active segment. For each plant, market, and segment, the amount of shipment placed within that segment must not exceed the indicator that marks that segment as active. Fifth, for each plant and market, at most one segment of the cost curve may be chosen as active. Across all segments, the selection indicators add up to no more than one. Finally, the total transportation cost adds up the priced shipment over every plant-market pair. For each pair, multiply that pair's cost coefficient by its square-root shipment measure, sum these across all plant-market pairs, and set the total cost equal to that sum.
